exercises.title: range() Variations
exercises.description: range() generates sequences of numbers. Practice range(stop), range(start, stop), and range(start, stop, step).
exercises.starterCode:
# range(stop): 0 to stop-1
print("range(5):")
for i in range(5):
    print(i, end=" ")
print()  # newline

# range(start, stop): start to stop-1
print("range(2, 7):")
for i in range(2, 7):
    print(i, end=" ")
print()

# range(start, stop, step): every step-th number
print("range(0, 20, 5):")
for i in range(0, 20, 5):
    print(i, end=" ")
print()
hints: range(5) produces 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — the stop value is NOT included.

exercises.title: Iterating Over a String
exercises.description: A for loop can iterate over any sequence, including strings. Print each character of 'Python' on its own line.
exercises.starterCode:
word = "Python"

# TODO: Use a for loop to print each character on its own line
for char in word:
    print(char)
exercises.expectedOutput:
P
y
t
h
o
n
hints: for char in word: iterates over each character in the string.
The loop variable char holds one character at a time.

exercises.title: Building a Multiplication Table Row
exercises.description: Use a for loop with range() to print the multiplication table for the number 7 (from 7×1 to 7×10).
exercises.starterCode:
number = 7

# TODO: Print the times table for 7, from 7x1 to 7x10
for multiplier in range(1, 11):
    result = number * multiplier
    print(f"{number} x {multiplier:2d} = {result:3d}")
hints: :2d formats an integer in a field 2 characters wide (right-aligned).
:3d formats an integer in a field 3 characters wide.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: for vs while
warmup.description: Use for when you know the number of iterations; use while when you don't. What does this print?
